# Lab 5: Mastering Control Structures in Java

## Introduction to Control Structures Lab

### Objective
In this lab, you will learn and practice using control structures in Java, including if-else statements, switch statements, and loops. By the end of this lab, you will be able to write Java programs that use control structures to make decisions, repeat tasks, and manage complexity.

### Prerequisites
- Basic understanding of Java syntax and data types
- Familiarity with Java Development Kit (JDK) and a Java IDE

---

### Step 1: Writing If-Else Statements

In this step, you will write a Java program that uses if-else statements to make decisions based on user input. Create a new Java project in your IDE and write the following code:
```java
// Import the Scanner class for user input
import java.util.Scanner;

public class IfElseExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create a Scanner object to read user input
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Prompt the user to enter their age
        System.out.print("Enter your age: ");
        int age = scanner.nextInt();

        // Use an if-else statement to determine if the user is eligible to vote
        if (age >= 18) {
            System.out.println("You are eligible to vote.");
        } else {
            System.out.println("You are not eligible to vote.");
        }

        // Close the Scanner object
        scanner.close();
    }
}
```
**Expected Result:**
The program will prompt the user to enter their age and then display a message indicating whether they are eligible to vote based on their age.

---

### Step 2: Writing Switch Statements

In this step, you will write a Java program that uses a switch statement to perform different actions based on user input. Create a new Java project in your IDE and write the following code:
```java
// Import the Scanner class for user input
import java.util.Scanner;

public class SwitchExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Create a Scanner object to read user input
        Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Prompt the user to enter a day of the week (1-7)
        System.out.print("Enter a day of the week (1-7): ");
        int day = scanner.nextInt();

        // Use a switch statement to determine the day of the week
        switch (day) {
            case 1:
                System.out.println("Monday");
                break;
            case 2:
                System.out.println("Tuesday");
                break;
            case 3:
                System.out.println("Wednesday");
                break;
            case 4:
                System.out.println("Thursday");
                break;
            case 5:
                System.out.println("Friday");
                break;
            case 6:
                System.out.println("Saturday");
                break;
            case 7:
                System.out.println("Sunday");
                break;
            default:
                System.out.println("Invalid day");
                break;
        }

        // Close the Scanner object
        scanner.close();
    }
}
```
**Expected Result:**
The program will prompt the user to enter a day of the week (1-7) and then display the corresponding day of the week.

---

### Step 3: Writing Loops

In this step, you will write a Java program that uses a loop to repeat a task. Create a new Java project in your IDE and write the following code:
```java
public class LoopExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Use a for loop to print the numbers 1-10
        for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
            System.out.println(i);
        }
    }
}
```
**Expected Result:**
The program will print the numbers 1-10 to the console.

---

### Step 4: Using Loop Control Statements

In this step, you will write a Java program that uses loop control statements to manage the flow of a loop. Create a new Java project in your IDE and write the following code:
```java
public class LoopControlExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Use a while loop to print the numbers 1-10, skipping 5
        int i = 1;
        while (i <= 10) {
            if (i == 5) {
                i++;
                continue;
            }
            System.out.println(i);
            i++;
        }
    }
}
```
**Expected Result:**
The program will print the numbers 1-10 to the console, skipping 5.

---

### Summary
In this lab, you learned and practiced using control structures in Java, including if-else statements, switch statements, and loops. You wrote Java programs that used these control structures to make decisions, repeat tasks, and manage complexity.

### Optional Challenge
Write a Java program that uses a combination of control structures to simulate a simple game, such as a quiz or a game of chance. Use if-else statements, switch statements, and loops to create a interactive and dynamic program.
